titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 22 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). In this letter, Spender discusses the report he is working on concerning the events surrounding July 1, and reflects on the odd fact that he is writing dispatches while his brother Rex is fighting in the trenches. He recounts a visit he recently received from a Brigadier-General Staff Officer seeking Spender's influence in order to secure a promotion. Spender expresses amusement that many officers think him influential over who gets promoted, given that his own career has little hope of progress. He mentions a holiday Lillian's maids are soon to take, and Lillian's hunt for a new house.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Aug. 22. 16. My pr The dispatch is not moving at all.
I am now back again at the 1st July which
I have just rewritten having found many
inaccuracies in my former account tho' based
on official documents! It really is difficult &
I was never intended as to be a historian but I hope
to buckle to it again better tho' I don't see
how I am ever to get up to date. The first
12 pages before the attack I am fairly well
pleased with now but than I was in personal
touch with the whole thing.
However I drew one winner today getting
my evening report passed as 'Excellent' I
feel as if I was back at School & had got
top marks on my essay. It really is curious
to be doing this kind of work in the middle of
a war with Rex in the trenches. You seem to have been having a happy time in
Wilts but I wonder whether you ought to have
spoken about Sam Hu not that I should mind
if he was found out, so I think it is as well. A very curious situation came about yesterday.
Gen' B.G a staff college contemporary had a talk
about the dispatch, & after I had settled down to
make notes came in & said What do you think
of my position as a Brigadier Gen' GS, kept on
the staff because of my qualifications & passed over
by 20 juniors in consequence. I am told it will
be all right after the war but you know what
that means. Can't you do anything? !!! When I add that he attributes his not having
gone quicker to the fact that he was anti Ulster
and therefore not acceptable to H.G you will
realise the humour of the situation. It never
struck me that I should be held responsible for
the promotions and awards in the Gazette, &
my future would indeed be a warm one if I
were not able to point to one great factor that
my name is not among them. I am so glad about the maids and hope
everything will go smoothly. The holiday for all
as the move certainly seems indicated now
& I think you can pay their tickets to Belfast
as a holiday treat (? from us both). I'm rather
disturbed at your having to house hunt again
you will be an experienced little pr when we
come to settle down in our own, whilst Maggie
ought to know exactly what is essential in
her department. I'm afraid St James Palace
sounds rather an expensive neighbourhood for a
pr. Was it the pr's face or the pr's furs which
made the porter chivy her to a first class carriage, or
the tout assemble perhaps? Mayne in Egypt
seems to be sorry he left the Division & finds 3
ASC's â 2 besides himself as altern of troop. Falls seems
rather low in the 36th simply stale I fancy. Quite enough for a pr even a pampered one.
when you next order at the store could you send out some
ANIS chocolate. No hurry about it. Has Rex written Paragraph completed on page 1
Written at the top of the first page page
